items.title: Canada captain Alphonso Davies sustains hamstring injury weeks before World Cup
items.summary_auto: Canada men's soccer captain Alphonso Davies sustained a left hamstring injury in Bayern Munich's Champions League semifinal against Paris Saint-Germain, putting his availability for the upcoming World Cup in doubt.

items.title: Canada's economy dropped 17,700 jobs in April as unemployment rose to six-month high
Canada's economy dropped 18,000 jobs in April as unemployment rose to 6-month high
items.summary_auto: Canada's unemployment ‌rate rose to a six-month high in April to 6.9 per cent as the economy lost a net of 17,700 jobs in March, Statistics Canada data showed on Friday, indicating a continued weakness in the labour market, which has struggled in the ​face of U.S tariffs and trade uncertainty.

items.title: U of T, OCAD amongst Ontario universities impacted by Canvas cyber breach
This ski hill wants to save its snow for next season. Can it be done?
items.summary_auto: For snow lovers in the Maritimes, this past winter was one of the best in years. Now, Nova Scotia's Ski Martock wants to save that snow for the slopes next season. CBC meteorologist Ryan Snoddon explains.

items.title: Spain prepares for evacuations as hantavirus-hit cruise ship heads for Canary Islands
items.summary_auto: Spanish authorities on Friday were preparing to receive more than 140 passengers and crew members on board a hantavirus-stricken cruise ship headed for the Canary Islands, where health officials have said they will perform careful evacuations.

items.title: Probe alleging 'El Chapo' sons fed victims to tigers led to case against top Mexican politician: court docs
items.summary_auto: While the Mexican government insists it needs more proof before acting on a U.S. request to hand over the former governor of Sinaloa, court records suggest the case against Rubén Rocha stems from a sprawling investigation into the sons of former Sinaloa cartel boss Joaquín “El Chapo” Guzmán Loera.

items.title: As AI creeps into telecoms, call centre agents worry they'll be replaced
items.summary_auto: Some call centre agents at a major Canadian telecom company say they fear for their jobs as AI takes an increasing presence in their workplace. A Telus employee in B.C. says a company requirement to use an AI "co-pilot" on phone calls, part of a suite of AI tools Telus uses internally, has her concerned she's training a system that will eventually take her job.

items.title: Ottawa to expand defence buying powers when national, economic security at stake
items.summary_auto: The Liberal government is preparing to give the new Defence Investment Agency broad authority to sidestep normal military procurement rules in cases tied to national and economic security. Proposed legislation would sharply expand exemptions for sole-source contracts while extending the agency’s influence into industrial policy, innovation and defence infrastructure.

items.title: Mortal Kombat II is brainless. That's why it works
items.summary_auto: Mortal Kombat is back for another round. But is what is clearly little more than a fan-service action-vehicle actually somehow good? A little bit. Maybe. Sort of?

items.title: Why do people still take cruises? Convenience, value outweigh health risks, experts say
items.summary_auto: By all accounts, the cruise industry is thriving, and tourism experts say they don't see that changing.

items.title: 'Mental well-being' biggest fear for thousands of seafarers stranded in the Strait of Hormuz
items.summary_auto: Food and water remain a significant concern for many of the thousands of seafarers stranded on vessels in the Strait of Hormuz. But the "the biggest fear for everybody is their mental well-being," says the general secretary of the International Transport Workers' Federation.

items.title: Live Nation, Ticketmaster vow to fight breakup after monopoly verdict
items.summary_auto: Live Nation sat down with the CBC’s Dave Seglins in the company’s first feature interview since a U.S. federal jury verdict found the entertainment giant abused its monopoly and overcharged fans.

items.title: Art and politics clash at Venice Biennale, as world conflicts upstage exhibition's opening
items.summary_auto: As protests, resignations and threatened legal action linked to Russia and Israel dominate the headlines in the lead-up to the Venice Biennale, a major exhibit puts artists from Africa and its diaspora at the heart of the conversation.

items.title: Liberals restore bill containing incoming GG's recommendation for Canada's military
items.summary_auto: On the same day the prime minister named Louise Arbour as Canada's next governor general, the Liberals voted to restore proposed legislation that includes Arbour's recommendation to strip the Canadian military of its power to investigate and prosecute sexual offences. Critics say the move ignores survivors, and could even represent a conflict of interest.

items.title: This woman’s identity was stolen in a CRA hack. Why hasn’t the impostor been charged in her case?
items.summary_auto: A B.C. nurse whose identity was stolen after her Canada Revenue Agency account was hacked says the CRA seems uninterested in pursuing the impostor who stole the public’s money after receiving a bogus refund in her name.

items.title: How the U.S. blockade is starting to hurt Iran's economy
items.summary_auto: Soon after he ordered a naval blockade in mid-April, U.S. President Donald Trump claimed Iran's pipelines would "explode" within a few days from the inability to export crude oil. While that extreme prediction didn't come true, there's evidence the blockade is starting to hurt Iran's economy.

items.title: A look back at David Attenborough's work as he turns 100
items.summary_auto: Britain's David Attenborough, who has for decades been the world's most authoritative voice on the natural world and whose documentaries have been watched by hundreds of millions, celebrates his 100th birthday today. Here's a look back at his work over the years.

items.title: Brockville, Ont., police arrest 17-year-old after finding 3 people dead in a home
items.summary_auto: Police in Brockville, Ont., said a 17-year-old is in custody for three counts of first-degree murder after finding three people dead in a home Thursday.

items.title: Montreal Victoire now 1 win away from Walter Cup Finals with Game 3 win over Frost
items.summary_auto: Maggie Flaherty and Hayley Scamurra scored 24 seconds apart early in the second period, Ann-Renee Desbiens made 27 saves, and the Montreal Victoire beat the Minnesota Frost 2-1 on Thursday night in the PWHL playoffs.

items.title: UBC, SFU among thousands of universities affected by cyber breach of learning software Canvas
items.summary_auto: The University of British Columbia and Simon Fraser University, B.C. 's two biggest universities by student enrolment, say a cyber breach of the Canvas learning software could affect students' personal information.

items.title: UBC, SFU among thousands of universities affected by Canvas software cyber breach
items.summary_auto: The University of British Columbia and Simon Fraser University, B.C. 's two biggest universities by student enrolment, say a cyber breach of the Canvas learning software could affect students' personal information.

items.title: Ontario estimated to lose  more than a third of its international students following permit cap: StatsCan
items.summary_auto: Ontario is set to lose more than a third of its international student population following the federal government's  student permit cap , preliminary estimates from Statistics Canada show.

items.title: U.S. says it intercepted Iranian attacks on 3 navy ships in Strait of Hormuz
items.summary_auto: The U.S. military said it intercepted Iranian attacks Thursday on three Navy ships in the Strait of Hormuz and "targeted Iranian military facilities responsible for attacking U.S. forces," highlighting the fragility of the month-old ceasefire between the two countries.

items.title: Man who sprayed U.S. Rep. Ilhan Omar from syringe during town hall pleads guilty to assault
items.summary_auto: A man who sprayed vinegar at Democratic U.S. Rep. Ilhan Omar at a town hall meeting in Minneapolis earlier this year pleaded guilty to assault Thursday in federal court after reaching a deal with prosecutors.

items.title: Tumbler Ridge Secondary School to be torn down and rebuilt after mass shooting, province says
items.summary_auto: The province says larger, more modular classrooms will be built in Tumbler Ridge in the coming weeks to use while plans for a permanent high school replacement are underway.

items.title: Rapper Kodak Black arrested in Florida on felony MDMA trafficking charge and pleads not guilty
items.summary_auto: Kodak Black was arrested this week on a drug trafficking charge in central Florida in his latest run-in with law enforcement. The rapper, whose real name is Bill Kapri, entered a not guilty plea.

items.title: U.S. trade court rules in favour of plaintiffs who challenged Trump's global tariffs
items.summary_auto: A U.S. trade court dealt another blow to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff strategy, ruling that his latest 10 per cent temporary global duties are unjustified under ‌a 1970s trade law, but blocked the levies for only two private importers and the state of Washington.

items.title: Bell fires employees it claims falsified attendance records, but some deny it
items.summary_auto: In an internal note obtained by CBC News, Bell said it investigated and terminated an undisclosed number of employees for “misrepresenting their presence in the workplace." But several fired employees, and the lawyer representing them, say they believe the employees were actually fired for economic reasons, rather than misconduct.

items.title: Toronto-based Sherritt pulls back from mining joint venture in Cuba amid expansion of U.S. sanctions
items.summary_auto: The United States on Thursday imposed ‌financial sanctions on a sprawling business conglomerate run by Cuba's military and a Cuban-Canadian mining joint venture, as the Trump administration ramps up pressure on the island's communist leaders by targeting sources of foreign investment.

items.title: Yves Veggie Cuisine making comeback with relaunch of 5 products
items.summary_auto: Maple Leaf Foods Inc. says it's reviving five Yves products, including veggie deli sliced products and original and Mexican veggie ground round.

items.title: London officer tells Ontario inquest the fatal police shooting of Sarnia tennis pro saved his life
items.summary_auto: London police Const. Ryan Hendrick tells an Ontario coroner's inquest that he thought he was going to die as Sarnia tennis player Justin Bourassa put him into a chokehold in 2021. Bourassa was fatally shot by another officer moments after the altercation.

items.title: Average rent across Canada fell by 5% in April, according to report
items.summary_auto: Rents in Canada have fallen back to the same level they were at three years ago, according to the new report from Rentals.ca and Urbanation. But average rents are still 21.9 per cent higher than the pandemic-era low of April 2021.

items.title: Elmer the kitten is now thriving after he was found in a bucket of glue
items.summary_auto: When staff at the Humane Society of North Texas first saw the scared little kitten, shaking and covered in industrial-strength glue, they didn't know if he would survive the night. But after roughly 24 hours of care, the little creature was free.

items.title: Maggie Kang, creator of KPop Demon Hunters, receives key to the city
items.summary_auto: The creator of the Oscar-winning animated film KPop Demon Hunters now holds a key to the city of Toronto, where she grew up.

items.title: Air Canada cuts more flights due to soaring jet fuel prices
items.summary_auto: Air travel continues to be impacted by soaring fuel prices. On Thursday, Air Canada confirmed that, due to the current high cost of jet fuel, it’s halting service earlier than planned on four seasonal flights to U.S. destinations.

items.title: Privacy watchdog finds thousands of tax account breaches, urges stronger protections
items.summary_auto: The federal privacy watchdog says there have been more than 42,000 breaches at the Canada Revenue Agency since 2020 as a result of people gaining unauthorized access to, or modifying, taxpayer information.

items.title: In meeting with Pope Leo, Rubio tries to minimize Trump's attacks
items.summary_auto: U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio met Pope Leo at the Vatican on Thursday morning, in a visit that was officially about diplomacy but in reality was about cleaning up after President Donald Trump’s latest swipes at the first American pope.

items.title: Elections Alberta issues cease-and-desist letters over voter info breach, privacy commissioner launches probe
items.summary_auto: Cease-and-desist letters have been sent to more than 500 Albertans who accessed a searchable database containing the personal information of millions of voters, while a new investigation into the breach has been announced by Alberta's privacy commissioner.

items.title: International Olympic Committee supports lifting ban on Belarusian athletes in international sports
items.summary_auto: In a political shift Thursday, the International Olympic Committee said athletes from Belarus should once again compete with their full national identity and not be vetted for neutral status, even as the war in Ukraine continues.

items.title: Fact check: Are wages rising faster than inflation?
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney has said wages are rising faster than inflation. CBC’s Fact Check team dives deeper into that claim

items.title: Wider mix of substances being discovered in Calgary drug supply, agencies see overdose spike
items.summary_auto: In testing over the past three months, University of Calgary researchers have found a wider variety of substances mixed together in the city's illicit drugs, which comes as several front-line agencies are responding to an increase in drug poisonings.

items.title: Procurement delays putting Mounties at risk, says union
items.summary_auto: More than a decade after the intended lifespan of their pistols lapsed, Mounties across Canada are getting a new sidearm — but their union says the significant delay shows why the RCMP's procurement process needs to be overhauled.
